articles.description: On 1 July, an Airbus A330 lifts off from Harare for London under the

articles.title: Zimbabwe's tobacco anchor in Britain holds at £129m
articles.description: Of every £100 of goods Zimbabwe sells to Britain, £96 is tobacco or beverages. While the wider UK–Zimbabwe trade relationship softened in the year to December 2025, Zimbabwean tobacco held its grip, and the IMF projects Zimbabwean GDP growth of 7.5% in 2025 and 5.0% in 2026. The product that built Zimbabwe's name as an exporter is still doing the work.

articles.title: The Apple of haircare gets its German cheque
articles.description: Ruka, the London-based textured-hair company founded by Zimbabwean entrepreneur Varaidzo Tendai Moyo and her co-founder Ugorji Agbai, has closed a $4.5m round co-led by Henkel Ventures and Freedom Trail Capital. The deal, which takes lifetime funding to roughly $10m and brings one of the world's largest consumer-goods groups onto the cap table, is the most meaningful endorsement yet of a Zimbabwean diaspora-founded brand.

more.title: Pardon Mapondera: gone but not forgotten at Venice
more.description: The fifth and final piece in our series on Zimbabwe's pavilion at the 61st Venice Biennale. Pardon Mapondera, the artist whose practice the room finally arranges itself around, treats discarded plastic and glass jars not as ecological signal but as ritual matter, and produces, in the process, the most quietly devotional installation in the Biennale this year.

more.title: Gideon Gomo: from stone to bronze, the ancestral algorithm at Venice
more.description: The fourth in a five-part series on Zimbabwe's pavilion at the 61st Venice Biennale. Gideon Gomo's first sustained body of bronze work, paired with discarded vehicle number plates and Zimbabwean springstone, is the move of a sculptor at full maturity rethinking the material vocabulary of his country's most internationally legible art form.

more.title: Shumba Maasai in Venice
more.description: At the 61st Biennale, the Zimbabwean artist's day-long procession through the city's campi, performed under the borrowed authority of a Murehwa chief, restages the social-sculpture lineage of Joseph Beuys for a contemporary African discursive practice.

more.title: Franklyn Dzingai: family albums and the neural commons at Venice
more.description: The third in a five-part series on Zimbabwe's pavilion at the 61st Venice Biennale. Franklyn Dzingai's reductive cardboard prints, built from the family album, the magazine clipping, the back-room studio photographs of mid-century Zimbabwe, turn out, on close looking, to be the most precisely engineered work in the room.

more.title: Felix Shumba: charcoal and the practice of disappearance at Venice
more.description: The second in a five-part series on Zimbabwe's pavilion at the 61st Venice Biennale. Felix Shumba's six-metre charcoal wall and his Telephone Reader series anchor a body of work that argues drawing is not the act of capturing the seen, but the act of staying present to what is on its way out of sight.

more.title: UK services share in Zimbabwe rises to 8.2%
more.description: The headline UK services trade with Zimbabwe softened year on year. The structural number underneath it tells the better story: Britain's share of all services Zimbabwe imports rose 2.4 percentage points to 8.2%, the highest measured level in years. As Zimbabwean buyers re-rate their global supplier mix, the UK is taking a bigger slice of the country's services demand.

more.title: Zimbabwe's demand lifts British exports by 14.6%
more.description: The Zimbabwean economy is in its strongest stretch in years: 7.5% GDP growth in 2025, a projected 5% in 2026, and that growth is pulling demand for cars, commercial vehicles, medicines and industrial machinery in with it. UK goods exports to Zimbabwe rose 14.6% on the year, the strongest line in an otherwise soft bilateral picture for Britain.

more.title: CAF A course returns as Zifa opens applications
more.description: Innocent Kurira innocent.kurira@chronicle.co.zw THE Zimbabwe Football Association (Zifa) has opened applications for the long-awaited CAF A Coaching Licence course, offering local coaches an opportunity to attain one of the continent's highest coaching qualifications without travelling outside the country. The course, scheduled for August, comes after a lengthy absence of the CAF A programme in Zimbabwe, a situation that had forced many aspiring elite coaches to seek the qualification in neighbouring countries at significant additional cost. Zifa has pegged the course fee at US$2,000. The return of the CAF A course is expected to be welcomed by coaches across the country, particularly those working in the Premier Soccer League and the country's various lower divisions, where the qualification is increasingly becoming a requirement for coaching at the highest level.

more.title: Liora Music urges resilience in love with new single
more.description: Mbulelo Mpofu mbulelo.smpofu@gmail.com Fresh from captivating audiences at the inaugural Imbawula Experience in Bulawayo, rising 3-step singer Liora Music is keeping the momentum alive with the release of her new single, Udlalile Ngami, a song that encourages listeners not to lose faith in love despite heartbreak and disappointment. The track explores the emotional pain of being betrayed or let down in relationships while carrying a message of hope, reminding people that one painful experience should not close the door to future love. According to the budding singer, the song is inspired by the belief that heartbreak should never define a person's future. “It doesn't matter how many times your heart gets broken and you get disappointed in love. You shouldn't give up on love because someone will come in and love you again and reciprocate the love you always give, she said. With conversations around emotional healing, self-worth and relationships continuing to resonate with many young people, Udlalile Ngami arrives with a message that is both timely and relatable. Liora Music’s growing profile received a major boost through her collaboration with acclaimed South African producer Blaq Major on Othandweni Kuyaphileka, a track that introduced her music to audiences beyond Zimbabwe and highlighted her potential on the regional stage. The collaboration followed her debut solo single, Ngathemba Wena, which introduced listeners to her artistic identity and showcased her ability to fuse heartfelt lyricism with the infectious rhythms of 3-step music. With Udlalile Ngami, Liora Music continues to build on that foundation. The single also serves as a preview of her debut EP, a project expected to showcase the full depth and versatility of her artistry. – Follow on X @MbuleloMpofu

more.title: Defending champions NUST through to Chancellor’s Games semis
more.description: Patience Dube dubepatiencet@gmail.com THE National University of Science and Technology (Nust) women’s darts team is through to the semi-finals of the Chancellor of Zimbabwe State Universities Staff Games at Great Zimbabwe University, Masvingo on Thursday. The team that consists of Tariro Mbofana, Brenda Ndlovu, Pretty Ncube, Mirriam Mudzingwa, Sichelesile Moyo, Noxolo Moyo and Candy Sibanda earned themselves a slot in the semi-finals against Lupane State University after winning all their games in the group games on Wednesday. At the competition which began on Sunday with 13 teams drawn into four pools whose group winners progressed to the semis, Nust emerged among the favourites. The Bulawayo ladies darts team who are the defending champions, accounted for Zimbabwe Open University 10-3, they beat Women’s University of Africa 10-2 and ended their group campaign with a 9-6 victory over ZIMCHE in a fixture that was described by Mbofana as tense. We played against ZIMCHE yesterday (Wednesday) and the game was tense because they were also good,” she admitted. “We also played against WUA in the quarter-finals which made us qualify for the semi-finals later today (Thursday),” she said. The ladies are ready to play against Lupane State University in a semi-final match today. University of Zimbabwe, Midlands State University, Bindura University of Science and Education, Chinhoyi University of Technology and Harare Institute of Technology, ZIMCHE, Women University of Africa and Zimbabwe Open University are the institutions taking part.
